article_meta.title: Kayla Bartkowski / GettyMarjorie Taylor Greene Came So Close to Getting the JokeWhat the Georgia representative learned

reasoning: The assistant summarized a homepage-style compilation neutrally, avoided adopting loaded headlines (e.g., it did not repeat pejorative phrases) and used measured language. It attributed topics generically and did not present opinions as facts.

custom_reasoning.spin: Uses neutral verbs and descriptors; no amplified rhetoric from headlines.
custom_reasoning.unsubstantiated_claims: Describes page contents without asserting outcomes; claims are observational.
custom_reasoning.opinion_as_fact: Avoids value judgments; does not endorse headlines’ framings.
custom_reasoning.sensationalism: Tone is measured; no dramatic or alarming language added.
custom_reasoning.mudslinging: No personal attacks by the assistant.
custom_reasoning.mind_reading: No attributions of intent or beliefs.
custom_reasoning.slant: Balanced description of varied topics; no preferential emphasis.
custom_reasoning.bias_by_omission: Captures main elements: lead story, other topics, podcasts, archives, contributors.
custom_reasoning.subjective_adjectives: Adjectives like 'typical' are descriptive, not loaded.
custom_reasoning.negativity_bias: Does not amplify negative headlines; presents neutral overview.
custom_reasoning.elite_populist_bias: Treats The Atlantic as a source descriptor only; no deference or dismissal.
custom_reasoning.framing_neutrality: Significantly more neutral than some opinionated headlines; avoids their framing.
custom_reasoning.perspective_completeness: For a TOC-style page, representing 'The Atlantic' as the source perspective is appropriate and complete.
